h1Title: Blastomycosis: Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis, and Treatment
heroOverview: Blastomycosis is a dimorphic fungal infection caused by Blastomyces dermatitidis, a mold that inhabits moist organic soil and decaying wood in forested regions near freshwater lakes and rivers in North America. Roughly 1,200-1,800 cases are reported annually in the United States, with hyperendemic foci in Wisconsin, Minnesota, Ontario, and the Mississippi and Ohio river valleys where incidence reaches 100 cases per 100,000 in some communities. After inhaling spores, roughly 50% of exposed individuals develop symptomatic pulmonary disease resembling pneumonia or influenza; unlike histoplasmosis, blastomycosis frequently disseminates to skin and bone even in immunocompetent individuals. Diagnosis is commonly delayed by 1-3 months because blastomycosis mimics bacterial pneumonia. Itraconazole treats the majority of non-critical cases; liposomal amphotericin B is reserved for severe disease.

keyStats.prevalence: Approximately 1,200-1,800 reported US cases per year; incidence up to 100 per 100,000 in hyperendemic Wisconsin and Ontario communities
keyStats.demographics: Adults aged 30-60 most commonly affected; men 2-4× more affected than women in most series; dogs co-infected at same exposures, serving as sentinel species
keyStats.avgAge: Peak incidence in adults aged 40-59; outdoor workers, hunters, and forestry workers at highest risk; cases rare in children under 5
keyStats.globalCases: Primarily North American but sporadic cases reported in Africa, India, and the Middle East from Blastomyces gilchristii and related species

definition: Acute pulmonary blastomycosis (ICD-10: B40.0) is the initial pulmonary manifestation of infection by Blastomyces dermatitidis, a thermally dimorphic fungus that grows as a white-to-tan mold at ambient temperature and converts to a large, thick-walled, broad-based budding yeast (8-15 µm) at 37°C. The fungus inhabits decomposing organic matter — moist soil, rotting wood, beaver dams, riverbanks — in North America's Great Lakes region, Mississippi and Ohio river valleys, and the St. Lawrence River basin. Infection begins when soil disturbance releases conidia that are inhaled into the alveoli. The host macrophage response attempts containment via granuloma formation, but Blastomyces yeast cells have a cell-wall carbohydrate (BAD1/WI-1) that suppresses TNF-α production, enabling immune evasion. Unlike histoplasmosis, which resolves spontaneously in the majority of immunocompetent individuals, blastomycosis progresses in roughly 25-40% of symptomatic cases to extrapulmonary dissemination — most commonly to skin, bone, and genitourinary tract. Infectious disease and pulmonology specialists manage the condition; dermatology consultation assists in recognizing and biopsying characteristic skin lesions that often provide an earlier diagnosis than respiratory workup alone.

typesClassification.description: Flu-like illness with fever, cough, and myalgia resolving spontaneously in immunocompetent individuals over 2-12 weeks. Often unrecognized and diagnosed retrospectively by serology. Comprises an estimated 50% of symptomatic cases.

Progressive lobar, multilobar, or bilateral infiltrates with fever, productive cough, and dyspnea not responding to antibiotics. Radiographically may mimic bacterial pneumonia, lung cancer, or sarcoidosis.

Bilateral miliary or diffuse infiltrates with rapid respiratory failure — rare but carries mortality of 40-89% even with treatment. Occurs after massive inoculum exposure or in severely immunocompromised hosts.

Extrapulmonary spread to skin (40-80% of disseminated cases), bone (10-30%), and genitourinary tract (10-30% in men). Can occur months after the initial pulmonary episode. Skin lesions often present as the patient's first concerning finding.

Involvement of brain parenchyma or meninges via hematogenous dissemination, occurring in 5-10% of disseminated cases. More frequent in immunocompromised patients. Requires prolonged voriconazole therapy due to superior CNS penetration.

primarySymptoms: Fever of 38-40°C, chills, and night sweats beginning 21-106 days after inhalation of spores — the incubation period is longer and more variable than most bacterial pneumonias.
Productive cough, often with purulent or blood-streaked sputum, persisting for weeks and failing to improve with courses of antibacterial antibiotics.
Dyspnea and pleuritic chest pain, present in moderate-to-severe pulmonary disease when multiple lung segments are involved, occasionally with pleural effusion.
Profound fatigue and myalgia that can be severe enough to cause a patient to seek hospital care after 1-3 weeks of declining function.
Skin lesions — verrucous (warty), crusted, or ulcerated plaques on sun-exposed areas (face, neck, forearms) in 40-80% of disseminated cases; these are often the most visible and alarming finding.
Bone pain, tenderness, and swelling in any skeletal site, with osteolytic lesions on X-ray or CT; vertebrae, ribs, and long bones are commonly involved in disseminated disease.
Scrotal or testicular swelling with or without pain from genitourinary dissemination in men; epididymitis is the most common genitourinary presentation.
Headache, confusion, focal neurological deficits, or meningism in CNS blastomycosis — an uncommon but severe complication more frequent in immunocompromised patients.
Weight loss and anorexia during prolonged undiagnosed disease — a 3-10 kg weight loss before diagnosis is common in retrospective series.
Laryngeal involvement producing hoarseness, stridor, or airway compromise — rare but important because it mimics laryngeal carcinoma.
earlyWarningSigns: Respiratory illness with fever failing to respond to two or more courses of antibacterial antibiotics in a patient from or returning from the Great Lakes region, Wisconsin, Minnesota, or the Mississippi valley
Slowly enlarging verrucous or crusted skin plaque on the face or arm without a dermatologic explanation — the most actionable early sign prompting biopsy
Radiographic mass or consolidation on chest imaging in a hunter, fisherman, or forestry worker from a hyperendemic area that does not resolve with antibiotics
Unexplained lytic bone lesion in a patient with concurrent or recent pulmonary illness from an endemic region
emergencySigns: Bilateral diffuse infiltrates with rapidly worsening hypoxia (O2 saturation <90%) — ARDS-pattern blastomycosis with mortality over 40% requiring urgent ICU-level care and IV liposomal amphotericin B
Altered mental status, focal neurological deficit, or nuchal rigidity in a patient with known or suspected blastomycosis — CNS involvement requiring urgent MRI brain and lumbar puncture
Acute urinary retention or progressive spinal cord compression from vertebral blastomycosis with epidural spread — surgical emergency alongside antifungal therapy
Rapidly expanding ulcerated skin lesion involving eyelid, lip, or airway threatening functional compromise — requires urgent culture, biopsy, and antifungal initiation

causes.cause: Inhalation of Blastomyces dermatitidis conidia from disturbed soil
causes.description: B. dermatitidis grows as a mold in moist acidic soil rich in decaying wood and organic matter. Disturbance by excavation, hunting, hiking, or natural flooding aerosolizes conidia (2-10 µm) that deposit in the alveoli and convert to pathogenic yeast. The specific soil niche is shared with beaver ponds, river banks, and lakeshores in the hyperendemic region.

causes.cause: Outdoor activities in hyperendemic forest and water environments
causes.description: Hunting, fishing, forestry work, camping near lakeshores, beaver dam excavation, and agricultural activities on sandy riverbanks are the dominant exposure mechanisms. Point-source outbreaks have followed beaver dam disturbance, earthwork along forested rivers, and group camping activities. Domestic dogs hunting in the same environments develop blastomycosis at rates paralleling local human cases.

Forestry workers, construction crews, land surveyors, and farm workers in the Great Lakes and Mississippi regions face repeated exposures. Wisconsin reported incidence of 2.5-3 per 100,000 overall with 20-fold higher rates among agricultural and forestry workers in some counties (Wisconsin Division of Public Health, 2019 data).

causes.cause: Immune suppression enabling dissemination from subclinical infection
causes.description: HIV infection, solid-organ transplantation, high-dose corticosteroids, TNF-α inhibitor therapy, and hematologic malignancy impair granuloma formation and allow fungal dissemination from pulmonary foci. In transplant recipients, blastomycosis is associated with 30-40% mortality; diagnosis is frequently delayed because skin lesions are attributed to drug reactions.

causes.cause: High-inoculum exposure at point-source environmental events
causes.description: Documented outbreaks following large beaver dam disruptions, excavation of contaminated soil, and flooding events have infected 40-70% of exposed individuals simultaneously, including immunocompetent outdoor workers. The largest recorded outbreak (Cass Lake, Minnesota, 1999) affected 55 individuals sharing a single environmental exposure.

riskFactors.factor: Residence in or travel to endemic regions
riskFactors.description: Wisconsin, Minnesota, Ontario, Michigan, and the Mississippi and Ohio river valleys account for the majority of US and Canadian cases. Hyperendemic zones in Wisconsin report 40-100 cases per 100,000 population in some counties. Travel to these regions for hunting, fishing, or camping constitutes significant transient risk.

Men account for 60-75% of cases in most US state surveillance reports, likely reflecting greater occupational and recreational exposure to soil in endemic regions rather than inherent biological susceptibility.

Forestry, farming, hunting, fishing, and construction in endemic areas confer estimated 5-20× the background community risk. Risk can be reduced by respiratory protection when conducting soil-disturbing activities.

riskFactors.factor: HIV/AIDS and other forms of immunosuppression
riskFactors.description: Immunocompromised individuals have higher rates of dissemination and mortality. HIV with CD4 below 200 cells/µL, solid-organ transplantation, and hematologic malignancy each increase dissemination risk substantially, though precise risk multipliers are not established from controlled studies.

Biologics that block TNF-α (infliximab, adalimumab, certolizumab) impair granuloma integrity, allowing reactivation or progressive primary blastomycosis. The FDA black-box warning for endemic fungal infections on TNF inhibitors specifically includes blastomycosis.

Dogs develop blastomycosis from the same environmental exposures as their owners and serve as sentinel animals for geographic risk zones. Canine cases often precede or accompany human cases from the same household or hunting party by 4-6 weeks.

Blastomycosis during pregnancy carries risk of congenital infection and placental transmission. Pregnancy alters immune tolerance; case series show a more fulminant course in pregnant women and potential for neonatal infection, requiring urgent antifungal management.

affectedDemographics: United States: highest reported incidence in Wisconsin (up to 3 per 100,000 statewide), Minnesota, Michigan, and along the Mississippi and Ohio river valleys. Wisconsin Division of Public Health reported 1,071 cases over a 10-year period.
Canada: Ontario, Manitoba, and Quebec report the majority of Canadian cases, concentrated in forested river-basin communities and First Nations communities in the north.
African blastomycosis: Blastomyces gilchristii and other Blastomyces species cause endemic infection in parts of East, Central, and West Africa; pathology resembles North American disease.
Occupational groups: hunting guides, forestry workers, and farmers in hyperendemic counties face substantially elevated incidence relative to urban residents in the same states.
diagnosisOverview:
Diagnosis of blastomycosis requires a high index of clinical suspicion because the condition is frequently mistaken for bacterial pneumonia, tuberculosis, or lung cancer — the average delay from symptom onset to correct diagnosis is 1-3 months. The diagnostic approach begins with exposure history: residence or travel in the Great Lakes region, Mississippi valley, or Ontario; outdoor activities (hunting, fishing, forestry, camping near rivers or beaver ponds); concurrent illness or death in hunting dogs.

The fastest route to diagnosis is direct visualization of the characteristic large, thick-walled, broad-based budding yeast on potassium hydroxide (KOH) preparation or calcofluor white stain of sputum, skin scrapings from lesion edges, or bronchoalveolar lavage fluid — sensitivity 36-60% for sputum and higher for BAL. Skin or bone biopsy showing pyogranulomatous inflammation with broad-based budding yeasts on Grocott methenamine silver (GMS) stain is definitive and histologically characteristic. Culture on Sabouraud's agar recovers the organism in 70-80% of cases but requires 2-5 weeks. Urine Blastomyces antigen EIA (cross-reactive with Histoplasma in 10-15% of cases) is positive in 89-93% of acute pulmonary and 99% of disseminated disease, providing rapid near-confirmation. Serology is less useful due to low sensitivity (50-70%) and cross-reactions. Chest CT may reveal consolidation, masses, tree-in-bud nodules, or miliary infiltrates. In suspected disseminated disease, MRI brain and bone scan or PET-CT identify extrapulmonary foci.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Rapid detection of polysaccharide antigen in urine — most sensitive single test for active disease
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A standard urine sample sent to a reference laboratory. Results in 1-3 days. Sensitivity 89-93% for pulmonary disease and up to 99% for disseminated blastomycosis. Cross-reacts with Histoplasma antigen in 10-15% of cases.

diagnosticTests.test: Direct microscopy (KOH/calcofluor white stain) of sputum or BAL
diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies broad-based budding yeasts on wet preparation — fastest way to detect fungal elements
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Sputum can be submitted immediately; BAL requires bronchoscopy under moderate sedation. Characteristic broad-based buds on KOH preparation are highly specific when seen by an experienced microscopist. Result within hours of sample processing.

diagnosticTests.test: Skin or bone biopsy with GMS staining
diagnosticTests.purpose: Histopathology of skin plaques or osteolytic lesions provides definitive diagnosis in disseminated disease
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A skin punch biopsy (3-4 mm) under local anesthesia takes under 10 minutes. Bone biopsy performed under CT guidance. GMS staining shows pyogranuloma with thick-walled yeast forms. Pathology report within 3-5 days.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Definitive species confirmation; necessary for antifungal susceptibility if azole failure suspected
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Sputum or biopsy specimen placed in fungal culture bottles or agar. Mycelial growth appears in 2-5 weeks at 25°C. Conversion to yeast at 37°C confirms B. dermatitidis. Positive in 70-80% of respiratory specimens from active disease.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Characterizes pulmonary infiltrates, cavities, mass-like consolidation, or miliary nodules; guides bronchoscopy
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: 15-minute scan; no oral or IV contrast typically needed unless masses require characterization. Report within hours at most institutions. Findings often described as mass-like consolidation (mimicking carcinoma) or alveolar consolidation.

diagnosticTests.test: MRI brain with and without gadolinium
diagnosticTests.purpose: Evaluates CNS blastomycosis with intracerebral abscesses, meningeal enhancement, or epidural spinal disease
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: 30-45 minutes in the scanner with IV contrast injected via small cannula. Nodular ring-enhancing lesions in brain parenchyma or diffuse meningeal enhancement suggest CNS involvement.

treatmentOverview:
Treatment of blastomycosis is guided by disease severity, site of involvement, and immune status, as summarized in the IDSA 2008 Clinical Practice Guidelines for Blastomycosis. Mild-to-moderate pulmonary blastomycosis in immunocompetent individuals is treated with oral itraconazole 200 mg three times daily for 3 days (loading), then 200 mg twice daily for 6-12 months. The prolonged duration (6-12 months vs 6-12 weeks for histoplasmosis) reflects blastomycosis's greater tendency to disseminate and relapse after shorter treatment courses. Itraconazole achieves MIC-adequate serum concentrations against B. dermatitidis and is well tolerated; therapeutic drug monitoring (trough >1 µg/mL) is recommended after 2 weeks.

Moderate-to-severe acute pulmonary disease — bilateral infiltrates, O2 saturation below 90%, or any degree of respiratory failure — is treated with intravenous liposomal amphotericin B 3-5 mg/kg/day for 1-2 weeks, followed by oral itraconazole to complete 12 months total. All-cause mortality from severe blastomycosis pneumonia is 40-89% without aggressive antifungal therapy; prompt initiation of amphotericin B reduces mortality substantially.

CNS blastomycosis requires liposomal amphotericin B 5 mg/kg/day for 4-6 weeks (achieving CNS penetration that itraconazole cannot match), followed by either voriconazole 200-400 mg twice daily or fluconazole 800 mg daily for at least 12 months. Bone blastomycosis responds well to itraconazole but may require 12-24 months. Genitourinary blastomycosis is treated identically to moderate pulmonary disease with 12 months of itraconazole. Immunocompromised patients of all types require initial liposomal amphotericin B induction regardless of clinical severity, followed by prolonged azole therapy.

medicalTreatments.name: Itraconazole (200 mg three times daily × 3 days, then 200 mg twice daily × 6-12 months)
medicalTreatments.description: First-line oral antifungal for mild-to-moderate pulmonary, skin, and bone blastomycosis. Take with meals or cola to maximize absorption. Therapeutic drug monitoring (trough >1 µg/mL at 2 weeks) recommended. Drug interactions with multiple medications including anticoagulants, statins, and immunosuppressants.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Response rates 90-95% for non-CNS blastomycosis when adequate serum levels achieved; relapse rate under 5% with full 12-month course

medicalTreatments.name: Liposomal amphotericin B (3-5 mg/kg/day IV, 1-2 weeks induction)
medicalTreatments.description: Preferred for severe acute pulmonary blastomycosis with respiratory failure, all CNS disease, all immunocompromised patients, and pregnant women. Lipid formulation preferred over deoxycholate to limit nephrotoxicity. Intravenous infusion over 2 hours in a monitored inpatient setting.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Mortality reduction from 40-89% to 20-40% in ARDS-severity disease; near-complete response in non-CNS disseminated disease

medicalTreatments.name: Voriconazole (200-400 mg twice daily orally or IV)
medicalTreatments.description: Preferred azole for CNS blastomycosis after amphotericin B induction due to superior CSF penetration compared with itraconazole. Also used as salvage therapy for itraconazole failures. Requires therapeutic drug monitoring (trough 2-5.5 µg/mL).
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: CNS response rates of 60-80% in case series; superior to fluconazole for CNS blastomycosis based on pharmacokinetic and clinical data

medicalTreatments.name: Fluconazole (400-800 mg daily orally)
medicalTreatments.description: Second-line alternative for non-CNS blastomycosis when itraconazole is not tolerated. Inferior in vitro and clinical activity compared with itraconazole; used as maintenance therapy in CNS disease after voriconazole or amphotericin B induction.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Response rate approximately 60-70% for non-CNS blastomycosis; less effective than itraconazole in head-to-head comparisons

medicalTreatments.name: Isavuconazole (372 mg loading × 6 doses then 372 mg daily)
medicalTreatments.description: Newer broad-spectrum triazole with activity against Blastomyces; less data than itraconazole or voriconazole. Potential role in patients unable to tolerate other azoles. Better drug interaction profile than itraconazole.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Limited clinical data; in vitro activity comparable to itraconazole; used as salvage when other azoles fail or not tolerated

surgicalOptions.name: Surgical debridement of osteomyelitis foci
surgicalOptions.description: Bone blastomycosis with necrotic cortical bone or sinus tracts may require surgical debridement alongside prolonged antifungal therapy. Spinal blastomycosis with epidural abscess causing neurological compromise requires urgent surgical decompression.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Good outcomes when combined with adequate antifungal therapy; spinal decompression neurological recovery dependent on duration of deficits before surgery

surgicalOptions.name: Resection of pulmonary mass or abscess
surgicalOptions.description: Rarely necessary; reserved for isolated pulmonary masses where diagnosis cannot be established non-invasively or which fail to respond to antifungal therapy. Blastomycosis lung masses can closely mimic primary lung malignancy.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Diagnostic yield near 100% from surgical resection; not needed for treatment in the vast majority of cases

whySeeSpecialist: An infectious disease specialist is essential for confirming diagnosis, choosing antifungal regimen and duration, managing toxicities, and monitoring treatment response with antigen levels and imaging. Misidentification as bacterial pneumonia, lung cancer, or tuberculosis by non-specialists is the leading cause of treatment delay in blastomycosis.
doctorSelectionGuide: Seek an infectious disease physician at a center with experience in endemic mycoses — particularly academic or regional referral hospitals in Wisconsin, Minnesota, or Ontario. For CNS or disseminated blastomycosis, specialist consultation is not optional; outcomes are significantly better when antifungal expertise guides induction and consolidation therapy.
hospitalCriteria: Any moderate-to-severe pulmonary presentation with O2 saturation below 92% or bilateral infiltrates
Suspected or confirmed CNS or disseminated blastomycosis requiring IV liposomal amphotericin B
Immunocompromised patient with suspected blastomycosis regardless of clinical severity
Hospital with bronchoscopy, CT-guided biopsy capability, and fungal culture laboratory
Pharmacy with IV antifungal formulations and therapeutic drug monitoring available
keyFacilities: Infectious disease specialty services at academic medical centers in the Great Lakes and Mississippi valley region
Dermatology services able to perform skin punch biopsies and GMS-stain histopathology
Orthopedic infectious disease units for bone blastomycosis management
preventionStrategies: Wear NIOSH-approved N-95 respirators when disturbing soil, excavating wood debris, or working near beaver dams in endemic areas.

Inform physicians about all recent outdoor activities in the Great Lakes or Mississippi valley regions when presenting with respiratory illness — this single piece of history accelerates diagnosis from months to days.
Avoid TNF-α inhibitor therapy in patients residing in hyperendemic areas without infectious disease evaluation and counseling about endemic mycosis risk.
Do not attribute persistent pneumonia failing antibiotics to antibiotic resistance before ruling out endemic fungi with antigen testing.
Monitor dogs that hunt or roam in endemic areas for respiratory illness or skin lesions, as canine cases frequently signal the same environmental exposure risk for owners.
lifestyleModifications: Complete the full 6-12 month itraconazole course without interruption — early stopping is the most common cause of relapse.
Take itraconazole capsules with a full meal or cola beverage; do not co-administer with acid-suppressing medications.
Attend monthly follow-up visits during the first 3 months, including urine antigen monitoring, to confirm treatment response.
Avoid further high-risk environmental exposures during treatment — while antifungals protect against progression, re-exposure adds fungal burden.
Monitor for signs of hepatotoxicity (jaundice, right upper quadrant pain, nausea) during itraconazole — liver function tests every 6 weeks during prolonged therapy.
Report any new skin lesions, bone pain, headaches, or visual changes during treatment promptly — these may signal new extrapulmonary dissemination.

recommended: Protein- and calorie-dense foods during the acute illness and recovery phase to offset infection-related catabolism
Cola beverages or acidic juices taken simultaneously with itraconazole capsules to enhance absorption
Adequate hydration (at least 2 L daily) to support renal function, especially with amphotericin B therapy
Vitamin D and calcium supplementation for patients with bone involvement or on prolonged antifungal therapy
avoid: Alcohol during antifungal therapy due to compounded hepatotoxicity risk
Grapefruit and grapefruit juice, which alter CYP3A4 metabolism and affect itraconazole and voriconazole levels
Acid-suppressing medications (PPIs, H2-blockers) taken simultaneously with itraconazole capsule formulation
High-fat meals immediately before IV amphotericin B infusions which may alter tolerability

exerciseGuidelines: Rest is recommended during febrile acute disease. Light activity (walking, stretching) can resume once fever resolves and respiratory function stabilizes, typically within 2-4 weeks. Patients with bone blastomycosis should restrict weight-bearing on affected sites per orthopedic guidance. Contact sports and vigorous exercise are deferred until imaging confirms lesion healing, usually at 3-6 months.
dailyManagement: Take itraconazole at fixed times with meals; use a pill organizer to avoid missed doses during the long treatment course.
Record any new symptoms — skin lesions, bone pain, neurological changes, or visual disturbance — in a symptom diary to report at each visit.
Measure weight weekly during the initial phase to detect continued catabolism suggesting inadequate treatment response.
Ensure adequate sleep and avoid immunosuppressants, including high-dose corticosteroids, unless medically essential during antifungal treatment.
Schedule and attend all follow-up antigen testing and imaging appointments — antigen normalization and chest CT improvement are the primary endpoints.
Notify the prescribing physician of any new medications to screen for drug interactions with itraconazole.
prognosis: Prognosis depends critically on severity at presentation and immune status. Mild-to-moderate acute pulmonary blastomycosis in immunocompetent individuals treated with a full course of itraconazole has an excellent prognosis, with response rates of 90-95% and relapse rates below 5%. Skin and bone blastomycosis respond well to prolonged itraconazole with healing of lesions over 3-6 months. Severe pulmonary blastomycosis with ARDS-level respiratory failure carries mortality of 40-89% even with treatment; outcomes improve substantially when liposomal amphotericin B is initiated promptly in an ICU setting. CNS blastomycosis has the worst prognosis, with mortality of 20-40% despite aggressive therapy, and significant neurological sequelae in survivors. Immunocompromised patients (transplant recipients, AIDS) have overall mortality 2-3× higher than immunocompetent patients for equivalent disease severity. Early diagnosis — before extrapulmonary dissemination — is the single most modifiable determinant of prognosis.
recoveryTimeline: Fever resolves within 1-3 weeks of antifungal initiation. Pulmonary infiltrates on CT improve over 4-12 weeks. Skin lesions begin healing at 4-8 weeks and fully resolve over 2-6 months. Bone lesions require 6-12 months to show radiographic healing. Urine antigen normalizes over 3-6 months in non-disseminated disease. Full antifungal course of 6-12 months is required regardless of symptom resolution.
complications: Dissemination to skin with verrucous and ulcerating plaques that can disfigure if left untreated for months — early antifungal therapy prevents scarring.
Skeletal blastomycosis with osteomyelitis and lytic lesions in vertebrae, ribs, or long bones — spinal disease can cause paraplegia from cord compression.
Respiratory failure (ARDS-pattern blastomycosis) with bilateral diffuse infiltrates and mortality of 40-89% — the most acutely lethal complication.
CNS blastomycosis with brain abscesses or meningitis carrying 20-40% mortality and neurological sequelae in survivors.
Genitourinary blastomycosis with chronic epididymo-orchitis, prostatic abscess, or tubo-ovarian abscess leading to infertility.
Relapse after incomplete treatment courses — occurring in 15-25% of patients who complete less than 6 months of therapy, often presenting months after apparent clinical resolution.

supportResources.name: IDSA Blastomycosis Practice Guidelines
supportResources.description: Infectious Diseases Society of America clinical practice guidelines with evidence-based treatment recommendations.

supportResources.name: CDC Blastomycosis Information
supportResources.description: Patient-facing information on symptoms, risk, testing, and treatment from the US Centers for Disease Control and Prevention.

supportResources.name: Wisconsin Division of Public Health — Blastomycosis Surveillance
supportResources.description: State epidemiology data, outbreak investigations, and veterinary blastomycosis information for the hyperendemic Wisconsin region.

confusedWithConditions.name: Histoplasmosis (acute pulmonary)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Both are dimorphic fungal pneumonias of the Ohio/Mississippi valley, but histoplasmosis rarely disseminates in immunocompetent hosts, while blastomycosis disseminates to skin and bone in up to 40% of symptomatic cases. Histoplasma produces small narrow-based buds; Blastomyces produces large, broad-based buds. Antigen tests are partly cross-reactive — culture or histopathology discriminates definitively.

confusedWithConditions.name: Tuberculosis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: TB and blastomycosis both cause chronic pneumonia with cavities, constitutional symptoms, and lymphadenopathy. TB shows acid-fast bacilli on sputum smear/culture and responds to rifampin-based therapy; blastomycosis requires antifungal therapy. CT chest in blastomycosis often shows a mass-like consolidation mimicking carcinoma, not seen in typical TB.

confusedWithConditions.name: Lung Cancer
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Pulmonary blastomycosis frequently presents as a mass or consolidation on CT that radiologically mimics primary lung carcinoma or lymphoma. Bronchoscopy with BAL cytology or skin/bone biopsy reveals pyogranulomatous inflammation with broad-based budding yeasts rather than malignant cells — the distinction critically alters management.

confusedWithConditions.name: Bacterial Pneumonia
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: The hallmark distinguishing blastomycosis from bacterial pneumonia is the absence of response to antibiotics after 1-2 courses. Hilar or mediastinal adenopathy and concurrent skin or bone lesions strongly suggest a fungal etiology. Urine Blastomyces antigen testing provides rapid near-confirmatory differentiation.

relatedConditions.name: Histoplasmosis (acute pulmonary)
relatedConditions.relevance: Sister dimorphic fungal infection co-endemic to the Ohio and Mississippi river valleys; both treated with itraconazole and amphotericin B.

relatedConditions.name: Coccidioidomycosis (Valley fever)
relatedConditions.relevance: Another North American endemic mycosis caused by a dimorphic fungus; geographically distinct (arid Southwest) but clinically similar acute presentation.

relatedConditions.name: Aspergillosis
relatedConditions.relevance: Another inhaled fungal pneumonia requiring antifungal therapy; both can cause severe disease in immunocompromised hosts.

relatedConditions.name: Pneumonia
relatedConditions.relevance: Blastomycosis is frequently misdiagnosed as bacterial pneumonia; both involve lung consolidation and fever requiring precise diagnosis to guide therapy.

faqs.question: What is blastomycosis?
faqs.answer: Blastomycosis is a fungal infection caused by Blastomyces dermatitidis, acquired by inhaling spores from moist soil and decaying wood near freshwater lakes and rivers in North America. Unlike most fungal infections, it disseminates to skin, bone, and other organs in a significant proportion of immunocompetent individuals.

faqs.question: What are the symptoms of blastomycosis?
faqs.answer: Symptoms include fever, productive cough with purulent or blood-streaked sputum, fatigue, and myalgia beginning 3-15 weeks after exposure. Disseminated disease causes verrucous or ulcerated skin plaques, bone pain, and testicular swelling. CNS involvement causes headache, confusion, or focal neurological deficits.

faqs.question: Where is blastomycosis found geographically?
faqs.answer: Blastomycosis is endemic to the US Great Lakes region and Ohio and Mississippi river valleys. Wisconsin, Minnesota, Michigan, and Ontario, Canada, report the highest incidence, with some Wisconsin counties exceeding 100 cases per 100,000 residents. Cases also occur in Africa and parts of Asia from related Blastomyces species.

faqs.question: How do you treat blastomycosis?
faqs.answer: Mild-to-moderate blastomycosis is treated with oral itraconazole 200 mg twice daily for 6-12 months. Severe disease, CNS involvement, or immunocompromised patients require intravenous liposomal amphotericin B for 1-2 weeks followed by itraconazole. A full 6-12 month course is needed to prevent relapse.

faqs.question: Is blastomycosis fatal?
faqs.answer: Mild blastomycosis in immunocompetent patients is rarely fatal with appropriate antifungal treatment. Severe pulmonary blastomycosis with respiratory failure carries mortality of 40-89% even with treatment. Immunocompromised patients and those with CNS involvement have the worst outcomes.

faqs.question: Can blastomycosis spread from person to person?
faqs.answer: No. Blastomycosis does not spread between people. Infection requires inhalation of fungal spores from soil or decaying wood. Dogs and humans can be infected simultaneously from the same environmental source, but neither transmits the infection to the other.

faqs.question: How is blastomycosis diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Diagnosis is confirmed by urine Blastomyces antigen testing (89-93% sensitive), direct microscopy showing broad-based budding yeasts in sputum or bronchoalveolar lavage, or biopsy of skin or bone lesions with silver stain histopathology. Fungal culture confirms species but takes 2-5 weeks to grow.

faqs.question: Why is blastomycosis often misdiagnosed?
faqs.answer: Blastomycosis mimics bacterial pneumonia, lung cancer, and tuberculosis on imaging. The average delay from symptom onset to correct diagnosis is 1-3 months. Clinicians outside endemic regions rarely consider it. The key clue is pneumonia failing to respond to two or more antibiotic courses in a patient with Great Lakes region exposure.

faqs.question: What does blastomycosis skin look like?
faqs.answer: Skin blastomycosis appears as verrucous (warty) crusted plaques with irregular borders, or as ulcerated lesions with heaped-up edges, typically on sun-exposed skin of the face, neck, or arms. The lesions grow slowly over weeks to months and can be mistaken for skin cancer or psoriasis.

faqs.question: How long does blastomycosis treatment take?
faqs.answer: Oral itraconazole is prescribed for 6-12 months for all forms of non-CNS blastomycosis. CNS disease requires amphotericin B induction for 4-6 weeks followed by oral azoles for at least 12 months. The long duration is necessary because blastomycosis has a high relapse rate after shorter courses.

faqs.question: Can dogs get blastomycosis?
faqs.answer: Yes. Dogs are highly susceptible to blastomycosis and develop respiratory and skin disease from the same soil exposures as their owners. Canine cases often precede or accompany human cases from the same household or hunting party. A dog diagnosed with blastomycosis should prompt evaluation of people with the same environmental exposures.

faqs.question: Does blastomycosis affect the brain?
faqs.answer: CNS blastomycosis occurs in 5-10% of disseminated cases, more commonly in immunocompromised patients. It presents as brain abscesses, meningitis, or spinal cord involvement. Treatment requires prolonged liposomal amphotericin B followed by voriconazole or fluconazole for at least 12 months. Mortality is 20-40% with aggressive treatment.

faqs.question: Is blastomycosis the same as histoplasmosis?
faqs.answer: No. Blastomycosis and histoplasmosis are separate fungal infections caused by different organisms, though both are endemic to the Ohio and Mississippi river valleys. Blastomycosis disseminates to skin and bone in immunocompetent hosts more frequently than histoplasmosis. Antigen tests cross-react — culture or biopsy provides definitive distinction.

faqs.question: Can blastomycosis cause bone disease?
faqs.answer: Yes. Bone blastomycosis occurs in 10-30% of disseminated cases, affecting vertebrae, ribs, skull, and long bones with osteolytic lesions visible on X-ray, CT, or MRI. Vertebral disease can compress the spinal cord causing paralysis. Bone disease requires at least 12 months of itraconazole and sometimes surgical debridement.

faqs.question: What follow-up is needed after blastomycosis treatment?
faqs.answer: Urine antigen levels are measured at 1, 3, 6, and 12 months to confirm response and detect relapse. Chest CT and relevant site imaging (bone scan, brain MRI if indicated) should be repeated at 3-6 months. Itraconazole serum levels are checked at 2 weeks to confirm therapeutic exposure.

metaDescription: Blastomycosis: fungal infection from North American forest soils that spreads to lungs, skin, and bone. Itraconazole treatment and prognosis reviewed.
